After:

The IDs swapped! But the positions make sense:
- id=10 was at [35,39], moved 5 times up-left → [30,34] ✓
- id=9 was at [34,15], unchanged → [34,15] ✓
So the IDs are just reassigned based on position (top-most, left-most ordering). The square I moved is now at [30,34]-[31,35], which is inside the blue circle area (blue circle is at [23,31]-[31,37]).
Looking at the image, I can see a magenta square inside the blue circle! The square is now overlapping with the blue circle.

Wait, this is a problem. id=10 is at col 15, but the circle starts at col 31. Moving up-left would take it further away from the circle horizontally.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the squares don't all move in the same direction. Maybe the movement direction depends on the square's position relative to the circle.
Actually, let me re-examine. id=10 was originally at [36,40] and I moved it 5 times to [31,35]. That's moving toward the circle (which is at cols 31-37). But id=9 was at [34,15] and I moved it twice to [32,13]... wait, no.

- I clicked it at [36,17] → moved to [35,16]
- I clicked it at [35,16] → moved to [34,15]
- Then I moved id=10 5 times
- After that, id=9 is at [30,34] and id=10 is at [34,15]
Wait, that doesn't make sense. id=9 was at [34,15] and now it's at [30,34]? That's not a diagonal up-left movement! [34,15] → [30,34] would be up 4, right 19. That's not consistent.

- id=10: [35, 39]-[36, 40]

- id=10: [34, 15]-[35, 16]

So the movement is consistent: clicking a magenta square moves it up 1, left 1.
Now, the square at [30,34] is inside the blue circle (which spans rows 23-31, cols 29-37). The square at [34,15] is far from the circle.
For the square at [34,15] to reach the circle, it would need to move up and right, not up and left. But clicking moves it up and left.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click the square at [34,15] to see movement direction
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 15}])
print("Result:", result)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nMagenta squares after:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
print("\nBlue circle:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        print(f"  pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
